title: A tale of two conjectures: from Mahler to Viterbo.
speaker: Yaron Ostrover
affiliation: Tel Aviv University; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: In this talk we explain how billiard dynamics can be used to relate a symplectic isoperimetric-type conjecture by Viterbo with an 80-years old open conjecture by Mahler regarding the volume product of convex bodies. The talk is based on a joint work with Shiri Artstein-Avidan and Roman Karasev.

proof_stages.label: Mahler volume product and minimizer problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the polar body and the volume product of a normed-space unit ball with its dual, states Blaschke--Santaló as the upper-extremal result, formulates Mahler's lower-bound conjecture, and explains why the conjectural non-unique Hanner-type minimizers make standard optimization strategies difficult.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic capacities and Viterbo's conjecture
proof_stages.summary: After recalling symplectic manifolds, non-squeezing, and the axioms and examples of symplectic capacities, the talk formulates Viterbo's volume-capacity conjecture for convex domains and summarizes known asymptotic and local evidence.

proof_stages.label: Product reduction linking Mahler and Viterbo
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: Mahler's conjecture is equivalent to the special case of Viterbo's conjecture for products B_X x B_X*, because a special symplectic capacity assigns all such products the value four, and the apparent mismatch of extremizers disappears symplectically.

proof_stages.label: Action-spectrum realization of Hofer--Zehnder capacity
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up characteristic foliations and closed characteristics on convex hypersurfaces, defines their action spectrum, and invokes Hofer--Zehnder theory to identify the minimal action with a symplectic capacity on convex domains.

proof_stages.label: Finsler-billiard translation of the product dynamics
proof_stages.summary: For the product of a convex body with its dual, the Hamiltonian dynamics on the boundary is interpreted as a Minkowski/Finsler billiard in the original body, with the billiard length matching symplectic action.

proof_stages.label: Two-bounce orbit and the value four
proof_stages.summary: In this special billiard geometry the shortest periodic trajectory is the two-periodic path from -q to q for any boundary point q, its length is four, and this proves the capacity computation that turns Viterbo's inequality into Mahler's conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Polytope formula toward Viterbo
proof_stages.summary: As recent progress, the talk describes a polytope result showing that a minimizing closed characteristic can be chosen to meet each facet at most once, leading to a largely combinatorial formula for the Hofer--Zehnder capacity and a possible route to Viterbo's conjecture.

examples.name: Hanner--Lima polytopes as many Mahler minimizers
examples.note: The family built from a segment by products and duals illustrates why ordinary convex-optimization attacks look implausible, and its later symplectic equivalence to balls motivates the 'symplectic glasses' viewpoint.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker describes Hanner--Lima polytopes as a large class built from segments by products and duals and states they share the cube’s Mahler volume, but does not compute or verify this on them.

passes.reason: They form a systematic infinite family attaining the conjectural Mahler minimum, used to defeat uniqueness/naive optimization intuition.

examples.name: Gromov non-squeezing ball-versus-cylinder phenomenon
examples.note: The attempted embedding of a large ball into a thin symplectic cylinder shows the obstruction that leads directly to defining symplectic capacities.

examples.name: Gromov width and cylindrical capacity
examples.note: These two capacities concretely instantiate the abstract axioms by measuring the largest symplectic ball inside a set and the smallest symplectic cylinder containing it.
examples.engagement_reason: Gromov width and cylindrical capacity are defined as standard examples of capacities and their extremal roles are stated, but no concrete capacity computation is carried out for them as examples.

examples.name: Rotated and random cubes with hard-to-compute capacity
examples.note: The cube, especially after rotation or random rotation, demonstrates how little is known about explicit symplectic sizes even for elementary convex bodies.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker works through cubes by giving the standard cube’s capacity, volume-obstruction bounds, asymptotics for special and random rotations, concentration, and an argument for the square-root upper bound via projection to a complex line.

passes.reason: Rotated cubes remain regular high-symmetry polytopes and are presented as adversarial hard cases for capacity computation.

examples.name: Liquid-crystal anisotropic-medium billiards
examples.note: The liquid-crystal analogy explains Finsler or Minkowski billiards as billiards in a homogeneous but direction-dependent medium.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces the Hamiltonian flow on the product body into a Finsler/Minkowski billiard system, explains the anisotropic reflection/length rule, and derives the two-bounce shortest orbit giving action four.
